worth_it_explanation: IAS Signal reports metrics across every channel the account runs, but the share of impressions it can actually measure varies by channel, format, and publisher integration status. A campaign that looks fully verified on the surface may have substantial volume on connected TV (CTV) or mobile in-app where measurement depends on publisher compliance with the Open Measurement (OM) SDK, or on social platforms where IAS operates through a platform integration rather than a direct tag. These checks confirm that the team understands which channels carry MRC-accredited measurement, where coverage gaps exist, and whether social platform reporting is interpreted within its actual scope rather than treated as equivalent to open-web verification.
intro: IAS Signal surfaces brand safety, viewability, and invalid traffic metrics for every campaign, but measurement coverage is not uniform across channels. On open-web display and video, IAS runs via a direct tag and its accreditation by the Media Rating Council (MRC) covers desktop, mobile web, mobile in-app, and CTV environments. In mobile apps, measurement depends on the publisher having integrated the Open Measurement SDK (OM SDK), the IAB Tech Lab standard that allows IAS and other verification vendors to run inside app environments without a browser context. On CTV, IAS holds MRC accreditation for viewability and sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT) filtration, but only for certified publisher traffic. On walled-garden social platforms, IAS operates through platform-specific integrations with Meta, TikTok, and YouTube rather than a direct tag, which limits the scope of what it can report. Understanding these distinctions is a prerequisite for reading IAS Signal data accurately.

audit_checks.title: CTV and mobile in-app measurement coverage gaps are not confirmed before reporting on those channels
audit_checks.impact_rationale: CTV and mobile in-app are among the fastest-growing channels in digital advertising budgets, but both carry structural measurement constraints that open-web display does not. Treating IAS Signal numbers from these environments as equivalent to open-web numbers, without understanding the coverage conditions, produces overstated confidence in the quality data for a substantial share of spend.

educate.base:
IAS holds Media Rating Council (MRC) accreditation for viewability and sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT) filtration across desktop, mobile web, mobile in-app, and CTV environments, but the conditions under which measurement operates in each environment differ.

In mobile in-app environments, IAS measurement depends on the publisher having integrated the OM SDK, the IAB Tech Lab standard that allows third-party verification vendors to receive measurement signals from within a mobile app without relying on a browser context. When a publisher app does not have an OM SDK integration, IAS cannot confirm viewability within that app. The IAB Tech Lab describes the OM SDK as the mechanism that enables sites and apps to send measurement signals to the Open Measurement Interface Definition (OMID) API. A campaign running in apps that lack OM SDK integration may still record impressions in IAS Signal, but the viewability data for those impressions will be absent or modeled rather than directly measured.

In CTV, IAS's MRC accreditation for SIVT filtration and viewable impressions applies to certified publisher traffic, meaning publishers who have completed CTV compliance certification with IAS. CTV inventory sourced through programmatic channels may include publishers at different stages of certification, and the coverage IAS can provide varies across that inventory. Server-side ad insertion (SSAI), the standard ad serving method for streaming video, restricts the ability to execute third-party scripts at the client device, which limits what verification signals can be collected in those environments.

A few signs this check applies:
  • IAS Signal shows a high measured viewability rate on CTV or mobile in-app line items, but the team has not confirmed whether the publishers delivering that inventory are OM SDK-integrated or CTV-certified
  • Post-campaign IAS Signal reports for CTV or mobile in-app line items show 'not measured' or low sample volumes in the viewability column for a subset of placements
  • The media plan includes significant CTV or mobile in-app budget but there is no note about IAS coverage conditions for those channels
You may want to confirm which publishers in the CTV and mobile in-app mix have completed OM SDK or IAS CTV certification before treating the viewability numbers from those channels as fully representative.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running CTV and mobile in-app campaigns alongside open-web display, you may want to confirm that the post-campaign quality report notes which channels carry direct IAS measurement and which carry modeled or partial coverage, so the viewability figures are not compared directly across channels without that context.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser with mobile in-app line items, you may want to confirm that the publishers delivering those line items have OM SDK integrations in place, so the IAS Signal viewability data reflects direct measurement rather than a gap in coverage.

learn_more.source_title: MRC digital accreditation: IAS accredited environments and metrics
MRC OTT/CTV and SSAI Digital Video Measurement Guidelines

impactful_updates.update_title: IAS earns MRC accreditation for SIVT filtration in CTV (April 2024)
impactful_updates.update_description: IAS received MRC accreditation for sophisticated invalid traffic filtration in connected TV environments, covering video impressions and viewable impressions. Accreditation applies to certified traffic only.

investigate.intro: In IAS Signal, review the channel and environment breakdown for the campaign to identify which line items delivered on CTV or mobile in-app, and what the viewability measurement status shows for those line items.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log into IAS Signal and navigate to the campaign in question. Open the 'Quality' or 'Viewability' report and filter or group by environment or channel.
Identify any line items or impressions tagged as CTV or mobile in-app. Note the measured viewability rate and compare it to the open-web display or mobile web line items in the same campaign.

Check whether any placements show 'not measured' or a low measurement rate in the viewability column. A gap between total impressions and measured impressions on a CTV or mobile in-app line item indicates that some inventory did not carry a viewability signal.

For mobile in-app line items, check the 'App' breakdown if available to see whether specific apps account for a disproportionate share of the unmeasured impressions. Those apps may not have OM SDK integrations.
Note the total impression volume on CTV and mobile in-app channels as a percentage of overall campaign delivery, so the coverage gap can be expressed relative to total spend.

evaluate.logic: For each CTV and mobile in-app line item, the practitioner records the measured viewability rate and the share of impressions marked 'not measured.' A line item where measured impressions match total impressions (or where the team has confirmed publisher OM SDK or CTV certification) passes. A line item with a material share of unmeasured impressions, or where the team cannot confirm certification status for the publishers delivering it, is a finding, recorded with the channel, impression volume, and measured versus total impression count.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the measured and total impression counts for each CTV and mobile in-app line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the CTV and mobile in-app line items on {{site_name}} carry measurement coverage gaps that have not been reviewed or documented. IAS viewability measurement in mobile apps depends on publishers having integrated the OM SDK; CTV measurement applies to certified publisher traffic. Line items with unmeasured impressions in those channels produce viewability figures that reflect only the measurable share of delivery. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the measured versus total impression split for each CTV and mobile in-app line item in IAS Signal, confirming publisher certification status for the channels with the largest unmeasured volumes, and noting coverage conditions in post-campaign reports so quality metrics are read with the appropriate context.

audit_checks.title: Social platform IAS measurement is treated as equivalent to open-web verification without accounting for its different scope
audit_checks.impact_rationale: IAS on Meta, TikTok, and YouTube operates through platform-specific integrations rather than a direct tag, which restricts what can be measured and how. Comparing IAS Signal quality metrics across social and open-web channels as if they were produced by the same method overstates the comprehensiveness of social verification and misreads the data.

educate.base:
On the open web, IAS measurement runs via a tag placed on the creative or the ad server, which gives IAS direct access to viewability signals, invalid traffic signals, and brand safety context at the impression level. On walled-garden social platforms, IAS does not place a tag in the same way. Instead, IAS operates through integrations built with each platform, where the platform shares data with IAS within the limits the platform allows.

For Meta, TikTok, and YouTube, IAS can measure metrics including viewability, invalid traffic, and brand safety or suitability, but the available signals and methodology differ from open-web measurement. The scope of what IAS can access on a social platform is shaped by what the platform exposes through its integration. For example, on some social environments IAS receives aggregated or sampled data rather than impression-level signals. The range of brand safety categories available for classification, and the granularity of placement-level reporting, also varies by platform and by the type of campaign objective.

Treating IAS Signal data from Meta, TikTok, or YouTube campaigns as directly comparable to IAS data from programmatic open-web campaigns introduces a methodological inconsistency. A higher measured viewability rate on a social channel relative to open web may reflect a difference in how viewability is counted in each environment rather than a genuine quality difference. Similarly, lower brand safety flags on a social channel may reflect a more limited classification scope rather than a cleaner content environment.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A post-campaign or in-flight report compares IAS viewability or invalid traffic rates across social and open-web channels side by side without a note about methodological differences
  • The account runs social campaigns through Meta, TikTok, or YouTube, and IAS Signal shows quality metrics for those channels, but the team has not confirmed what integration method IAS is using on each platform
  • Brand safety reporting from IAS on a social platform is used to draw conclusions equivalent to those drawn from open-web brand safety measurement
You may want to confirm with IAS documentation or your IAS account team what signals are available for each social platform integration before comparing quality metrics across channels.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running campaigns across both programmatic open web and Meta or TikTok, you may want to confirm that the channel-level quality comparison in post-campaign reports notes that IAS measurement methodology differs between those environments, so the team does not draw conclusions about relative channel quality from figures that are not methodologically equivalent.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser with significant social spend alongside open-web display, you may want to confirm that the brand safety and viewability benchmarks used for each social platform reflect the scope of what IAS can measure on that platform, rather than assuming the same classification depth as open-web placements.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Ad Verification Supplement: Enhanced Content Level Context and Brand Safety

investigate.intro: In IAS Signal, identify which campaigns or line items are delivering on social platforms and review what quality metrics IAS is reporting for those channels compared to open-web placements.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log into IAS Signal and navigate to the campaign or account-level quality report. Filter or segment by channel or publisher to identify line items delivering on Meta, TikTok, YouTube, or other social platforms.

Note which quality metrics (viewability, invalid traffic, brand safety, or suitability) are populated for social line items versus open-web line items. Identify any metrics that are present for open-web placements but absent for social placements.

If a cross-channel comparison report exists (for example, a campaign summary that shows viewability rates by channel), review whether the report includes a footnote or methodology note explaining that social platform measurement runs through a platform integration rather than a direct tag.

Ask the team or IAS account representative whether the social platform integrations in use are IAS direct integrations (e.g., Meta direct, TikTok direct) or pass-through, and which metrics each integration surfaces at the impression level versus the aggregate level.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which social platforms are active in the account, which IAS quality metrics are reported for each, and whether any cross-channel comparison report treats social and open-web metrics as directly comparable without a methodology note. An account that uses IAS social measurement and documents the scope of each platform integration, and that flags methodological differences in any cross-channel report, passes. An account where social and open-web IAS metrics are compared without qualification is a finding, recorded with the platforms involved and the specific metrics being compared.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the social platforms active in the account, the IAS metrics reported for each, and whether any cross-channel quality comparison is in use above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that IAS quality metrics from social platforms on {{site_name}} are reported alongside open-web metrics without distinguishing that the measurement methodology differs between environments. On Meta, TikTok, and YouTube, IAS operates through platform integrations that restrict the scope of available signals compared to a direct open-web tag. Treating those figures as equivalent overstates the comprehensiveness of social verification. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the IAS integration method for each social platform in use, confirming which quality metrics are available at the impression level versus the aggregate level, and adding a methodology note to any cross-channel quality comparison so the team reads the data within its actual scope.

audit_checks.title: IAS measurement coverage is not reviewed by format within each channel, so channels with mixed measured and unmeasured formats are read as fully verified
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A channel can deliver a mix of formats, some carrying full IAS measurement and some carrying partial or no measurement. Rolling all formats together into a single channel-level quality rate obscures the fact that a portion of that channel's delivery is not verified, which produces an inflated composite figure.

educate.base:
IAS measurement coverage varies not only by channel but by ad format within a channel. A single programmatic buy may include display banner, outstream video, and native formats running across the same set of publishers. IAS can confirm viewability for a display banner using standard pixel-based measurement. For outstream video, viewability measurement requires the player to expose a viewability signal that IAS can read, which depends on the publisher's player implementation. For native formats, the measurement approach again depends on whether the publisher has implemented the signals IAS needs to classify the placement.

On mobile in-app channels, the OM SDK described by the IAB Tech Lab as the standard for enabling third-party measurement in app environments supports display, video, and native ad formats, but each requires the publisher to implement format-specific integration within the SDK. A publisher app may have integrated OM SDK for display but not yet for native or outstream video, so a campaign running multiple formats within that app will have uneven coverage across formats.

IAS Signal reporting can be segmented by format and by placement in addition to by channel. When a channel-level quality summary is the only view in use, format-specific coverage gaps are absorbed into the aggregate rate without appearing as a distinct signal. A campaign showing 70 percent viewability on a mobile in-app channel may reflect 85 percent viewability on display formats and zero measurement on native formats, producing the composite figure from a partial base.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The IAS Signal report for a campaign shows channel-level quality metrics but no breakdown by ad format within each channel
  • The campaign includes outstream video, native, or rich media formats alongside standard display in the same line items or channels, and no format-level quality review has been done
  • The account runs mobile in-app campaigns across multiple formats, and the IAS Signal report does not separate display from video or native delivery within those line items
You may want to review IAS Signal at the format level for any channel that includes more than one ad format, so coverage gaps for specific formats are visible rather than absorbed into a channel composite.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running both display and native ad formats within mobile in-app placements, you may want to confirm that the IAS Signal format breakdown is reviewed separately for each format, so a coverage gap on native does not reduce confidence in the display viewability figures on the same channel.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using outstream video alongside display in programmatic buys, you may want to confirm that the IAS Signal viewability rate reported for the open-web display and video line items is broken out by format. Outstream video measurement depends on player implementation and may differ from display viewability on the same publishers.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Mobile Application (In-App) Advertising Measurement Guidelines

investigate.intro: In IAS Signal, segment the campaign quality report by ad format within each channel to identify whether coverage rates differ across display, video, and native formats running in the same channel.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log into IAS Signal and open the quality or viewability report for the campaign. Look for a dimension or breakdown option that allows grouping by format or creative type in addition to channel or environment.

For each channel with significant impression volume, segment the report by format (display, video, native, or rich media) and compare the measured viewability rate and the share of measured impressions across formats.

Identify any format within a channel that shows a materially lower measurement rate than other formats on the same channel. Note whether the gap is consistent with a publisher integration issue (e.g., one specific app or publisher accounting for most of the unmeasured volume in a particular format).

If IAS Signal does not offer a format-level breakdown directly, check whether the ad server or demand-side platform (DSP) report can provide impression volume by format for cross-referencing with the IAS channel-level quality data.

evaluate.logic: For each channel carrying more than one ad format, the practitioner records whether a format-level breakdown of IAS quality metrics has been reviewed. A channel where the format-level breakdown confirms equivalent or understood coverage rates across formats passes. A channel where a format-level review has not been done, or where the review reveals a material difference in coverage between formats that has not been documented, is a finding, recorded with the channel, the formats present, and the coverage rates by format where available.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the channels and formats in use and whether a format-level IAS quality breakdown has been reviewed for each above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that IAS quality reporting for {{site_name}}'s campaigns is reviewed at the channel level without a format-level breakdown, which absorbs format-specific coverage gaps into the composite channel rate. When display, video, and native formats run within the same channel, coverage rates can differ across formats depending on publisher implementation, and a channel composite can reflect a much smaller measured base than the total delivery suggests. {{voice_recommend}} segmenting IAS Signal by format within each channel that carries more than one format type, documenting the measured impression share for each format separately, and noting any format with a material coverage gap so it can be raised with the publisher or supply source.
